figures.caption: Table 2: SpatialBabel-QA-Score (%) under four inference modes on SpatialBabel-Primitive-QA (100 scenes × 8 programmatic questions = 800 per cell). best_cc / worst_cc use each model’s primitive-best / primitive-worst scene-code language from Table 1 restricted to the 4-language subset {Three.js, Canonical JSON, Blender Python, Scene Language DSL}. Bold = row max. All cells use the CoT-symmetric evaluator (Appendix C).

Table 7: Accuracy (%) under four inference modes on CV-Bench-2D (1,438 items) and CV-Bench-3D (1,200 items). best_cc and worst_cc use each model’s primitive-best and primitive-worst scenecode language from Table 1, matching the convention of Tables 2 and 6. Bold = row maximum within each benchmark. The Qwen3-VL-8B CV-Bench-2D best_cc cell (Canonical JSON, its primitivebest) is left blank because the full 1,438-item run was not completed in time; the corresponding CV-Bench-3D cell (1,200 items) was run and is reported.

Table 11: Per-question-category accuracy (%) for Qwen3-VL-8B base vs single-view S3-FT on SpatialBabel-QA (100 scenes × 8 questions = 800 questions per cell, distributed across the 5 categories). ∆ is in percentage points. Bold marks rows where S3-FT improves by more than 5 percentage points.

Table 12: Single-view S3-FT on Qwen3-VL-8B across multiple independent random seeds. direct, nl_cot, best_code_cot (threejs) are 3-seed runs (0, 42, 1337); worst_code_cot (canonical_json) is a 2-seed run (the seed-0 checkpoint was trained with an older configuration not directly comparable on the longest-output mode).

Figure 1: Geometric primitives as a spatial language for VLMs: the paradox and two approaches to exploit it. (A) The paradox: A VLM produces a faithful 3D primitive-reconstruction of the input image in code, yet fails simple spatial questions on the same scene (B) Code-CoT (Section 4) closes the gap at inference time: the model first reconstructs the scene as primitive-based executable code, then reasons over its own structured output. (C) S3-FT (Section 5) closes the gap at training time: the model’s own primitive-reconstruction outputs are deterministically parsed into structured spatial annotations, which serve as self-supervised labels for LoRA fine-tuning, requiring no human labels. Training on primitives alone yields gains on real-photo benchmarks.
Figure 3: The three-phase S3-FT pipeline (Phase 1: pseudo-GT generation; Phase 2: structured annotation extraction; Phase 3: multi-task LoRA fine-tuning).

Table 6: SpatialBabel-Hypersim-QA-Score (%) under four inference modes. best_cc / worst_cc use each model’s primitive-best / primitive-worst scene-code language from Table 1. ∆best-direct = best_cc− direct (pp). Bold = row max.

Figure 5: Per-scene-code SpatialBabel-Reconstruct-Score on primitive scenes under Direct prompting per model. Proprietary APIs are in rows 1 and 2; open-weights bases in rows 3 and 4. Green bars = best language per row, red bars = worst per row, blue bars = other (matching Table 1’s bold/red convention). Same data as Table 1.

Figure 6: Language difficulty rankings per model (baseline, primitive scenes). Each cell shows the model’s rank among the 6 languages (1 = best) and absolute F1. Models disagree on which languages are easy or hard, and the ranking pattern correlates with model scale: smaller models (Qwen2.5-VL-3B, LLaVA-OV-7B) rank high-exposure languages above low-exposure ones, whereas larger models show the opposite pattern driven primarily by Canonical JSON being structurally simple.

Table 14: Single-view S3-FT on Hypersim (20 held-out scenes × 8 questions = 160 per cell), reported as accuracy in %; ∆ is in percentage points. Under the CoT-symmetric evaluator, S3-FT lifts direct and NL-CoT modes for both Qwen models on Hypersim, but Code-CoT modes mostly regress; Qwen3-VL-8B code-CoT canonical_json collapses (23.6% → 3.2%) due to the model emitting near-empty Three.js code under the rare canonical_json prompt format that does not appear in the S3-FT training distribution.

Figure 4: S3-FT vs. Baseline on two base VLMs. Panel 1: SpatialBabel-Primitive-Reconstruct mean F1 (in-distribution). Panel 2: SpatialBabel-Primitive-QA accuracy. Panel 3: IFEval. Panel 4: mean across 12 standard VLM benchmarks (out-of-distribution).

Figure 8: Primitive vs. Hypersim per-scene-code Reconstruct Score (best of Direct/NL-CoT) for the three models with both splits. Hypersim numbers are FOV-filtered (full per-component metrics computed against FOV-matched pairs), matching Table 8. Hypersim scenes are substantially harder across all languages, but the per-language profile is preserved: cross-language gaps are driven by language familiarity rather than scene content.

Figure 7: Same scene, same model (Gemini-2.5-Pro), four scene-code languages. Top row: 3D render of the ground truth and of each predicted scene, reconstructed from the model’s generated code and rendered with the GT camera. Bottom row: top-down (x,z) layout; dashed rings = GT positions, filled markers = predicted positions. Gemini’s Unity C# output (F1=0.86) recovers the scene with slightly different object placement than its Three.js output (F1=1.00) despite both describing the same image.

Table 10: Per-model comparison of Reconstruct-Score F1 (Hungarian-matched object F1, averaged across the six scene-code languages, Direct prompting) against SpatialBabel-Primitive-QA accuracy on the relationship category and overall (100 scenes × 8 questions, Direct prompting). Across the nine VLMs evaluated on both, Reconstruct F1 spans 0.439 to 0.937 (a 2.13× ratio) while QA-relationship clusters within a 2.5-pp band. Pearson r and Spearman ρ at the bottom; per-scene-code correlations of Reconstruct F1 against QA-relationship report which scene-code languages’ reconstruction skill is most predictive of relational reasoning.

Table 4: General VLM performance across 12 standard benchmarks. Numbers are accuracy (%); parenthesized values are signed deltas versus the corresponding base model (green = improvement, red = regression). Bold = best per row per base model. † MMMU re-scored with a regex letter extractor (validation split, n=900) instead of VLMEvalKit’s default LLM-judge for base→S3-FT consistency; small deltas on this row are within re-scoring noise. S3-FT is trained only on primitive images, yet gains transfer to real-photo benchmarks.
Figure 9: Per-language F1 on 100 primitive held-out scenes for the two base VLMs, comparing Baseline, v1 (collapsed), single-view S3-FT (our self-supervised recipe) and cross-viewpoint S3-FT.

Table 9: SpatialBabel-Reconstruct-Score components, per (model, mode), averaged across the six scene-code languages on primitive scenes. Parse = parse rate; F1 = Hungarian-matched object F1; ClsAcc = class accuracy on matched pairs; PosFid = 1− clipped pos_rmse_norm; ScaleFid = 1− clipped scale_rmse.
